quiz_questions.question_text: Which is larger: static or kinetic friction coefficient?

options.text: Static friction coefficient (μₛ)
They are always equal
Kinetic friction coefficient (μₖ)
It depends on the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Static friction prevents motion: f_s ≤ μ_s·N. It adjusts up to its maximum value (μ_s·N). Static friction coefficient is always ≥ kinetic. The answer is 'Static friction coefficient (μₛ)'. The other options — 'Kinetic friction coefficient (μₖ)', 'They are always equal', and 'It depends on the surface' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the normal force?

options.text: The resistive force that opposes sliding between two surfaces
The perpendicular contact force a surface exerts on an object
The external push or pull applied by an agent on an object
The gravitational pull of Earth acting on the object's mass

quiz_questions.explanation: The normal force is the contact force perpendicular to a surface. On a flat surface: N = mg. On an incline: N = mg·cos(θ). It adjusts to prevent objects from passing through surfaces. The answer is 'The perpendicular contact force a surface exerts on an object'.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is tension?

options.text: The gravitational pull acting on an object toward Earth's center
The pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable
The drag force exerted by air on a moving object's surface
A compressive contact force that pushes an object from behind

quiz_questions.explanation: Tension Tension is the pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable. In an ideal (massless, inextensible) string, tension is the same throughout. The other options — 'A compressive contact force that pushes an object from behind', 'The gravitational pull acting on an object toward Earth's center', and 'The drag force exerted by air on a moving object's surface' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: If N = 50 N and μₖ = 0.3, what is the kinetic friction force?
quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic friction opposes motion: f_k = μ_k·N, where μ_k is the kinetic friction coefficient and N is the normal force. The answer is '15 N'. The other options — '50 N', '150 N', and '0.006 N' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why does it take more force to start an object moving than to keep it moving?

options.text: Objects gain mass at rest
Kinetic friction is larger
Gravity increases at rest
Static friction (max) is greater than kinetic friction

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic friction (fₖ) : Acts on a sliding object. fₖ = μₖN (constant). μₛ > μₖ — it takes more force to start motion than to maintain it. The other options — 'Gravity increases at rest', 'Kinetic friction is larger', and 'Objects gain mass at rest' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: In an ideal pulley, what happens to tension?

options.text: It changes direction but not magnitude
It doubles due to mechanical advantage
It drops to zero at the axle point
It is reduced to half by the pulley

quiz_questions.explanation: A pulley changes the direction of tension but not its magnitude (ideal pulley). In an ideal (massless, inextensible) string, tension is the same throughout. The other options — 'It doubles due to mechanical advantage', 'It is reduced to half by the pulley', and 'It drops to zero at the axle point' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

quiz_questions.question_text: For connected objects with a taut string, what do they share?

options.text: The same force
The same mass
The same velocity always
The same acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: When two objects are connected by a taut, inextensible string, they must move together and share the same acceleration. They do not necessarily have the same force acting on them (different masses), but the string constrains them to accelerate equally.

quiz_questions.question_text: What direction does friction act on a sliding object?

options.text: In the same direction as the object's motion
Opposite to the direction of the object's motion
Perpendicular to the surface the object slides on
Straight downward toward the center of the Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic friction always acts in the direction opposite to the motion of the sliding object. It resists the sliding motion and works to slow the object down. This is true regardless of whether the surface is flat or inclined.

quiz_questions.question_text: What two factors determine the strength of the friction force?

options.text: The object's speed and the surface temperature
The friction coefficient and the normal force
The object's mass and the applied force on it
The object's color and the surface roughness alone

quiz_questions.explanation: The friction force is calculated as f = μN, where μ is the coefficient of friction (depends on the surfaces) and N is the normal force (depends on the weight and orientation). These two factors together determine friction's strength.

quiz_questions.question_text: In an ideal massless string, is the tension the same everywhere along the string?

options.text: No — tension is greatest at the point where force is applied
Yes — tension is uniform throughout an ideal massless string
No — tension decreases as you move farther from the pulling end
Only if the string is perfectly horizontal and not at an angle

quiz_questions.explanation: In an ideal (massless, inextensible) string, the tension is the same at every point along the string. This is because a massless string requires zero net force to accelerate, so the tension must be equal on both ends.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'friction' in physics?

options.text: A force that opposes relative motion or attempted motion between two surfaces
A pulling force transmitted through a rope, string, or cable between objects
The perpendicular contact force that a surface exerts on a resting object
The gravitational force that pulls all objects toward the center of Earth

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction is a contact force that opposes the relative motion (or attempted motion) between two surfaces in contact. It can be static (preventing motion) or kinetic (opposing ongoing sliding). Friction depends on the surfaces and the normal force.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'static friction' mean?

options.text: The friction force that acts on an object that is currently sliding
The friction force that prevents an object from starting to move
The normal force that acts perpendicular to the surface of contact
The tension force in a rope when it is holding an object at rest

quiz_questions.explanation: Static friction is the friction force that acts on a stationary object to prevent it from starting to move. It adjusts its magnitude up to a maximum value (fₛ,max = μₛN). Once the applied force exceeds this maximum, the object begins to slide.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'kinetic friction' mean?

options.text: The friction that acts on an object that is already sliding across a surface
The friction that prevents a stationary object from beginning to move
The gravitational force pulling an object along a surface toward Earth
The tension force that keeps an object attached to a rope or cable

quiz_questions.explanation: Kinetic friction acts on a moving (sliding) object and opposes the direction of motion. It has a constant value for given surfaces: fₖ = μₖN. Kinetic friction is always less than or equal to the maximum static friction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the symbol 'μ' (mu) represent in friction equations?

options.text: The coefficient of friction between two surfaces in contact
The mass of the object that is sliding across the surface
The gravitational acceleration acting on the object near Earth
The velocity at which the object moves across the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: The Greek letter μ (mu) represents the coefficient of friction. It is a dimensionless number that depends on the types of surfaces in contact. A higher μ means more friction. There are two types: μₛ (static) and μₖ (kinetic).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'tension force' mean?

options.text: A pulling force transmitted along a rope, string, or cable when taut
A pushing force applied by a surface perpendicular to the object
A resistive force that opposes the sliding motion of two objects
A force created by compression when two objects are squeezed together

quiz_questions.explanation: Tension is a pulling force transmitted through a flexible connector such as a rope, string, cable, or chain when it is pulled taut. Tension always pulls — it cannot push. In an ideal string, tension is the same throughout.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'coefficient of friction' describe?

options.text: A number that describes how much friction exists between two specific surfaces
The total weight of the object pressing down on the surface below it
The speed at which an object slides across a particular surface type
The angle at which an object begins to slide down an inclined plane

quiz_questions.explanation: The coefficient of friction is a dimensionless number that characterizes the friction between two specific surfaces. Rubber on concrete has a high coefficient (~0.6-0.8), while ice on ice has a low coefficient (~0.03). It depends only on the surface materials.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'ideal string' or 'ideal rope' mean in physics problems?

options.text: A string that has no mass and does not stretch under any applied force
A string made of the strongest possible material available on Earth
A string that can only transmit pushing forces between two objects
A string that absorbs all the tension so the objects feel no force

quiz_questions.explanation: An ideal string is a simplification used in physics: it is massless and inextensible (does not stretch). This means the tension is the same throughout the string and it transmits forces instantaneously between connected objects.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'pulley' refer to in physics?

options.text: A wheel with a groove that redirects the direction of tension in a rope
A flat surface that increases the friction between an object and a rope
A spring mechanism that stores energy when a rope is pulled on it
A type of knot used to connect two ropes together at their ends

quiz_questions.explanation: A pulley is a simple machine consisting of a wheel with a groove around it. A rope runs through the groove. An ideal pulley changes the direction of the tension force without changing its magnitude, making it easier to lift objects.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'normal force' on a flat surface?

options.text: The perpendicular support force a surface exerts, equal to mg on a flat surface
The frictional force between the object and the surface it rests upon
The gravitational pull of Earth on an object resting on the surface
The applied force a person uses to push an object across the surface

quiz_questions.explanation: On a flat horizontal surface, the normal force equals the object's weight (N = mg). The normal force is always perpendicular to the surface and prevents the object from passing through. On inclines, N = mg cos θ.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the phrase 'opposes relative motion' mean regarding friction?

options.text: Friction acts in the direction opposite to sliding or attempted sliding
Friction prevents all objects from moving under any circumstances
Friction only acts on objects that are not touching any surface at all
Friction increases the speed of objects moving across rough surfaces

quiz_questions.explanation: Friction opposes relative motion — it acts in the direction opposite to the way the surfaces slide (or would slide) against each other. If a box slides to the right, kinetic friction acts to the left. If you push a box to the right but it does not move, static friction acts to the left.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student tries to push a heavy desk across the floor but it does not move. The student pushes with 40 N. What is the static friction force?

options.text: 0 N because the desk is not moving across the floor
40 N in the direction opposite to the student's push
80 N because static friction is always double the applied force
20 N because friction is always half of the applied pushing force

quiz_questions.explanation: Since the desk does not move, it is in equilibrium and the net force is zero. Static friction adjusts to exactly match the applied force: 40 N opposing the push. Static friction can range from 0 up to its maximum value (μₛN).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student slides a 10 kg box across a floor where μₖ = 0.2. What is the kinetic friction force? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 2 N

quiz_questions.explanation: Normal force N = mg = 10 kg × 10 m/s² = 100 N. Kinetic friction fₖ = μₖN = 0.2 × 100 N = 20 N. The kinetic friction force on the sliding box is 20 N, opposing the direction of motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student hangs a 5 kg weight from a rope attached to the ceiling. What is the tension in the rope? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 5 N

quiz_questions.explanation: The weight hangs in equilibrium, so tension equals weight. T = mg = 5 kg × 10 m/s² = 50 N. The rope must pull upward with 50 N to balance the 50 N downward pull of gravity on the weight.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student tries to slide a wooden block across a table. The block does not move until the student pushes with more than 12 N. Once sliding, only 8 N is needed to keep it moving at constant speed. What are the maximum static and kinetic friction forces?

options.text: Static: 8 N, Kinetic: 12 N
Static: 12 N, Kinetic: 8 N
Static: 20 N, Kinetic: 4 N
Static: 10 N, Kinetic: 10 N

quiz_questions.explanation: The block starts moving when the push exceeds 12 N, so the maximum static friction is 12 N. Once sliding, it takes 8 N to maintain constant speed (net force = 0), so kinetic friction is 8 N. This confirms that μₛ > μₖ.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student uses a pulley to lift a 3 kg mass by pulling down on a rope. Assuming an ideal pulley, how much force must the student apply? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 15 N downward on the rope
30 N downward on the rope
60 N downward on the rope
3 N downward on the rope

quiz_questions.explanation: An ideal single fixed pulley changes the direction of the force but not its magnitude. The weight is mg = 3 kg × 10 m/s² = 30 N. The student must pull with 30 N downward to lift the 30 N weight upward.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student pulls a sled across snowy ground at constant speed with a horizontal rope. The tension in the rope is 25 N. What is the friction force on the sled?

options.text: 0 N because the sled is moving at constant speed
25 N opposing the direction of the sled's motion
50 N opposing the direction of the sled's motion
12.5 N in the same direction as the sled's motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Constant speed means zero acceleration, so the net horizontal force is zero. The 25 N tension pulls the sled forward, so friction must be exactly 25 N backward to balance it. The forces are balanced even though the sled is moving.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student connects two blocks with a string on a frictionless table. Block A (2 kg) is on the table and Block B (3 kg) hangs off the edge. What is the acceleration of the system? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 10 m/s²
6 m/s²
15 m/s²
4 m/s²

quiz_questions.explanation: The driving force is the weight of Block B: F = 3 kg × 10 m/s² = 30 N. The total mass of the system is 2 kg + 3 kg = 5 kg. Using F = ma: a = 30 N / 5 kg = 6 m/s². Both blocks share this acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that a car's tires screech when braking suddenly on dry pavement but slide quietly on ice. What explains the difference in friction?

options.text: Ice has a much lower coefficient of friction than dry pavement
The car weighs less on ice because ice is a colder surface temperature
The tires change shape on ice so they contact less surface area overall
Gravity is weaker on icy surfaces due to the water molecules in the ice

quiz_questions.explanation: The coefficient of friction between rubber and ice (~0.1) is much lower than between rubber and dry pavement (~0.7). Since friction force = μN, a lower coefficient means much less friction, which is why cars slide more easily on ice.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student uses a rope to pull a 15 kg crate across a rough floor at constant velocity. The coefficient of kinetic friction is 0.4. What tension must the student apply? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

options.text: 6 N

quiz_questions.explanation: At constant velocity, net force = 0, so tension = friction. N = mg = 15 × 10 = 150 N. Friction = μₖN = 0.4 × 150 = 60 N. The student must pull with 60 N of tension to maintain constant speed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student observes that a hockey puck slides much farther on ice than a rubber ball rolls on a sidewalk when both are given the same initial push. What is the primary reason?

options.text: The puck is heavier than the rubber ball so it has more momentum
Ice has a much lower coefficient of friction than the concrete sidewalk
The puck is flatter and cuts through the air with much less resistance
The sidewalk absorbs more heat from the ball, slowing it down faster

quiz_questions.explanation: The coefficient of friction between the puck and ice is very low (~0.03), while the coefficient between rubber and concrete is much higher (~0.6-0.8). Lower friction means less opposing force, so the puck decelerates much more slowly and travels farther.
